title: How Brika works
subtitle: The six pieces that make up everything in Brika: plugins, blocks, bricks, sparks, workflows, and boards.

plugin.title: Plugin
plugin.definition: A self-contained process that extends Brika. Plugins bring everything else: the blocks, sparks, bricks, and pages you'll use to build automations. Each one is a regular package.json plus code.
plugin.example: Install @brika/plugin-timer and you immediately get timer and countdown blocks plus a dashboard brick.

block.title: Block
block.definition: A piece of compute that runs inside a plugin. Each block has typed inputs, outputs, and a config. Blocks are the parts you wire together when you build a workflow.
block.example: The timer plugin's Countdown block has a 'duration' config and emits a 'completed' output when it finishes.

brick.title: Brick
brick.definition: A widget that lives on a board. Plugins push live data into their bricks, and you configure each one to your liking.
brick.example: The timer plugin's Timers Dashboard brick shows all your active timers as a widget on a board.

spark.title: Spark
spark.definition: An event with a schema. Plugins fire sparks when something interesting happens. Workflows and other plugins listen for them and react.
spark.example: When a countdown begins, the timer plugin fires a 'timerStarted' spark carrying the name, duration, and start time.

workflow.title: Workflow
workflow.definition: A pipeline of blocks defined in YAML. Brika runs through it, routing data from one block to the next and firing sparks along the way.
workflow.example: When the sun sets, turn on the lights. Wait thirty minutes. Then turn off the TV.

board.title: Board
board.definition: A page of bricks. You drop them in, configure them, and arrange the layout however you like.
board.example: A 'Living Room' board with weather, timer, and electricity-consumption bricks all in one view.
